segments.s419-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed before the reporting verb for emphasis. گفت is the simple past 3rd‑person verb “he said” (subject is implied from context). با + proper names (با رستم اسفندیار) is an older/poetic use meaning “to/with” — here marking the addressee (i.e. ‘he spoke to Rostam Esfandiyār’).
segments.s419-g2: که‌ای is a poetic/vocative address (originally que + ey → ‘o’) used to call someone; this form is not captured by the ordinary sense list, so it functions as ‘O!’. نیک‌دل (compound adjective) and مهتر (honorific = chief/elder) qualify the addressee, and نامدار is an adjective placed after those titles meaning “renowned/illustrious.” The sequence is a vocative string addressing Rostam.
segments.s419-g3: من is the first‑person pronoun “I.” ایدون is an archaic/poetic particle (here supporting the statement; its exact sense isn’t in the provided list). شنیدستم is an old first‑person perfect/past form meaning “I heard / I have heard.” از introduces the source “from,” and بخردان is a plural/collective meaning “the wise ones.”
segments.s419-g4: This is a coordinated phrase giving the sources: بزرگان “the great ones/elders,” و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” بیداردل is a compound adjective meaning “wakeful‑hearted / vigilant,” and موبدان is the plural of موبد (mōbed), the Zoroastrian priest — i.e. ‘the priests.’ The list names the groups from whom the speaker heard the information.
segments.s419-g5: ازان is the archaic contraction of از + آن meaning “from them/that.” برگذشته is an adjectival/participial compound meaning “having passed” or “long‑past/long‑departed.” نیاکان is the plural noun “ancestors,” and تو (‘you’) marks possession (your ancestors). Word order places the modifying phrase before the noun it qualifies.
segments.s419-g6: A three‑term noun phrase describing those ancestors: سرافراز “honored/proud,” و coordinating, دین‌دار “pious/devout,” و again, پاکان “the pure (plural).” The final تو repeats the possessive ’your’ for emphasis — a poetic doubling common in classical Persian.
segments.s419-g7: که begins a subordinate clause “that / for,” linking back to the previous statement. دستان here literally means “hands” but in poetic usage can imply line/branch/offspring; it is followed by the compound adjective بدگوهر (“bad‑gem,” i.e. evil‑natured) modifying دیوزاد (“demon‑born”). Together this phrase describes those of demonic birth and foul nature.
segments.s419-g8: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase “in the world” (به used poetically where modern Persian would use در). فزونی ندارد is a negated present‑tense construction literally “does not have increase/superiority” (ندارد is 3rd‑person present of داشتن). نژاد (placed after the verb for emphasis) is the noun “lineage/race,” so the clause says that no such demon‑born stock has a lineage in the world that is superior.
segments.s419-g9: فراوان is an adverb/adjective meaning 'many' or 'plentifully' modifying the following verb phrase. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source (سامش = سام + possessive -ش, 'from Sam'). نهان داشتند is a compound: نهان ('hidden') plus داشتند (past 'they had/kept'); together 'they kept hidden'. Word order is typical older Persian: quantity/source + adjective + past-tense verb.
segments.s419-g10: همی is an adverb here meaning 'ever/continually' emphasizing the action. رستخیز (literary) means 'uprising' or 'raising' (here a tumult/insurrection rather than the theological 'resurrection'). جهان is the object 'the world'. داشتند is past tense 'they had/maintained' — together 'they continually maintained (or had) an uprising of the world.'
segments.s419-g11: تنش is تن + -ش (possessive) 'his body'. تیره is an adjective 'dark' modifying تنش. بد موی is a collocation where بد ('bad/poorly') qualifies موی ('hair') — 'ill‑haired' or 'with unseemly hair'. و 'and' connects the two descriptions; رویش is روی + -ش 'his face', modified by سپید 'white' (pale). Word order places possessive suffixes on the nouns and links qualities with و.
segments.s419-g12: چو is the conjunction 'when'. دیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the object suffix -ش ('saw him'). دل سام is a genitive phrase 'Sam's heart' (دل + proper name following it), شد is past 'became', and ناامید is the resulting adjective 'hopeless/despondent'. The clause order (verb before subject phrase) is poetic but the meaning is 'when he saw him, Sam's heart became hopeless.'
segments.s419-g13: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded' (courtly diction). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). پیش دریا literally 'before/at the sea' (پیش used as 'in the presence of' or 'toward'), and برند is the subjunctive/optative 3rd‑plural of بردن 'to carry/take' — 'that they take/carry (it) to the sea.'
segments.s419-g14: مگر here introduces a hope/expectation ('perhaps' / 'that maybe') and links the purpose clause to what is hoped for. مرغ و ماهی 'the birds and the fish' are the subjects of the following verb. ورا = و + را (here written as a single word) means 'to it/him' (the thing being carried). بشکرند is the present/subjunctive 3rd‑plural of شکر کردن 'to give thanks' — 'that the birds and fish may thank it.' (In older Persian مَگر can carry this sense of 'perhaps/in order that'.)
segments.s419-g15: بیامد is the simple past 'it/he came.' بگسترد is the (poetic/archaic) past form 'spread' (here 'spread its wing'); the verb precedes the noun object in this poetic order: 'it came, (and) it spread the Simurgh's wing' (read as 'the Simurgh spread its wing'). سیمرغ is the proper name; پر is the object 'wing/feather.'
segments.s419-g16: ندید is the past with negative prefix ن- here meaning 'did not see' (classical past negation). اندرو = اندر + او 'in it / within it' (adverbial phrase). هیچ is the negative quantifier 'no/none'. آیین و فر 'rite (custom) and far (royal glory)' are the two noun complements — so the clause means 'in it he saw no ritual nor glory.' Note the negative verb governs the whole following nominal phrase.
segments.s419-g17: ببردش is a past transitive verb with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'): literally 'he carried him'. به جایی که begins a prepositional phrase with a relative clause introduced by که ('that/where'). بودش is بود + the enclitic -ش and here functions as 'it was' (referring to the place as belonging/being his); کنام is a poetic noun 'bosom/lap' functioning as the predicate complement. Word order is verb → destination phrase → relative clause with predicate.
segments.s419-g18: This clause uses the preposition ز ('from') plus دستان ('hands') and the first‑person pronoun مر ('me/my') to mark source: 'from my hands'. او را is the definite object marker + pronoun 'for him' (or 'him' as beneficiary). خورش is a noun 'food/dish' and بود کام is a copular construction: بود (past 'was') + کام 'satisfaction/fulfilment' — literally 'the food was fulfilment for him'. The order is poetic: source → beneficiary → theme → copula + predicate.
segments.s419-g19: اگر چند here functions concessively ('although/though a little'), with چند modifying ناهار to mean 'a small/limited lunch'. سیمرغ is the subject and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause therefore means 'though the Simorgh's meal was little.'
segments.s419-g20: تن زال ('Zal's body/person') is the topic; پیش اندرش is a locative/relational phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of it' (پیش = in the presence of, اندر + ش = 'in/within + its'). خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised/worthless' and بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole reads 'Zal's body, in its presence, was despised.'
segments.s419-g21: بینداختش is the past tense of بینداختن 'to throw/cast' with enclitic -ش 'him' ('he threw him'). پس is an adverb 'then/after that'. به پیش کنام is a directional/locative phrase 'before/in front of the bosom' (پیش = before/in the presence of; کنام = bosom/lap). The clause describes a completed action: 'then he cast him before the bosom.'
segments.s419-g22: به دیدار او is a prepositional phrase 'at/for the sight/meeting of him' (i.e., 'on seeing him'). کس نبد شادکام uses نبد (negative past 'was not') with کس here meaning 'anyone/no one' in the negative sense, so the phrase means 'no one was glad/fortunate.' The structure is adverbial phrase → negated predicate.
segments.s419-g23: همی خورد is a habitual/progressive expression ('kept eating' or 'was eating continually'); خورد is the simple past of خوردن 'to eat'. افگنده modifies مردار: افگنده مردار = 'the cast/throw‑away carrion' (participial adjective). اوی is a classical enclitic pronoun meaning 'his/its' or 'he/it'. The clause reads 'he kept eating the carrion that was cast (to him).'
segments.s419-g24: ز جامه برهنه is a causal/figurative phrase 'from his garment (i.e., with his garment gone), naked' — جامه برهنه = 'garmentless/naked'. تن خوار اوی places the predicate after the noun: 'his body (تن) was despised (خوار),' with اوی a possessive 'his'. Poetic word order omits a copula but implies 'he was despised, his garment torn and his body naked.'
segments.s419-g33: The clause begins with the enclitic/object pronoun 'ورا' (to him/it) before the verbs, a common poetic order. 'برکشیدند' is a past, 3rd-person plural verb meaning 'they raised' (here physically or exalted him), followed by the coordinating 'و' and the past verb 'دادند' 'they gave'. The noun 'چیز' follows the verbs as the (indefinite) direct object 'a thing(s)', an inverted order used in poetry where the modifiers come next.
segments.s419-g34: 'فراوان' adjectivally (or adverbially) qualifies the preceding 'چیز' as 'many/much'. The sequence 'برین سال' is a prepositional/time phrase: 'بر' + archaic demonstrative 'ین' = 'in/that (this) year'. 'بگذشت' is past 3rd‑person singular 'passed' (of time), and 'نیز' adds 'also/too'. Together this gives the temporal remark 'that year, too, passed'.
segments.s419-g35: The noun phrase begins with 'یکی' ('a certain'), followed by the noun 'سرو' (a cypress, a poetic image for a tall tree or beauty). The adjectives 'بد نابسوده' qualify it: 'بد' = 'bad/ill' here functioning as an intensifier of a negative/untouched state and 'نابسوده' = 'untouched/unwithered'. The possessive clitic 'ش' appended to 'سر' marks 'its top/head' (i.e. the crown of the tree). The whole phrase describes the tree's top.
segments.s419-g36: The sentence begins with the temporal conjunction 'چو' = 'when'. 'با شاخ شد' literally 'became with a branch' (i.e. 'put forth a branch' or 'grew a bough'); 'شد' is simple past/past participle 'became/was'. 'رستم آمد' is the main clause: 'Rostam came' (past 3rd sg). The final 'برش' is a clitic-like expression meaning 'to/onto it' (he came toward/at it); this is syntactically the goal of 'آمد'.
segments.s419-g37: The preposition 'ز' (poetic for 'from/out of') introduces causes or sources. A coordination of nouns follows: 'مردی' here refers to 'manliness/valour', 'بالا' to stature or loftiness, and 'دیدار اوی' is 'his appearance/sight' with the enclitic 'اوی' marking possession ('his'). The conjunctions 'و' connect the listed attributes, so the phrase enumerates qualities 'from/of his manliness and loftiness and his appearance'.
segments.s419-g38: 'به گردون برآمد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'arose/ascended to the sky/heavens' with 'برآمد' (past 3rd sg) as the verb and 'به گردون' the direction/goal ('to heaven'). 'چنین کار اوی' is a nominal phrase 'such a deed of his' (here placed after the verb for poetic word order), where 'چنین' = 'such', 'کار' = 'deed/affair', and 'اوی' is the possessive 'his'.
segments.s419-g39: 'برین گونه' means 'in this manner/thus' (the prepositional 'بر' with the archaic demonstrative 'ین' + 'گونه' = 'way/manner'). 'پادشاهی گرفت' is a simple past clause where 'پادشاهی' = 'kingship' is the object/what is obtained and 'گرفت' = 'he took/obtained' (past 3rd sg). The structure states that by these means he took the kingship.
segments.s419-g40: 'ببالید' is past 3rd‑person singular meaning 'he became proud' (or 'he grew haughty'). The conjunction 'و' links the next clause 'ناپارسایی گرفت' where the abstract noun 'ناپارسایی' ('impiety/moral unworthiness') is the thing that 'گرفت' ('seized/took'); in Persian this construction expresses that he was seized by or fell into impiety — i.e. he became impious.
segments.s419-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. افگند is a past tense verb (from افکندن) meaning 'cast/laid upon'; in this construction it takes مِهر (affection) as the thing cast and بر + زال marks the target ('upon Zāl'). Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before subject and object), a common poetic ordering: 'افگند (the Simurgh) مهر بر زال' = 'the Simurgh cast affection upon Zāl.'
segments.s419-g26: This clause describes the sky/heavens changing state. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'; برو here functions adverbially/colloquially with the verb (thereupon/then + go/turn) — a poetic pairing meaning 'thereupon it turned.' زین گونه = 'in this manner' (زین = 'this/from this' in older/poetic usage, گونه = 'manner'), and چندی سپهر = 'for a while (the) sky' with سپهر (the heavens) placed after the time phrase by poetic inversion.
segments.s419-g27: ازان پس که is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter, when'; که introduces the subordinate clause. مردار چندی چشید is a past action: چشید (tasted/experienced) with مردار (carrion) as its object and چندی 'for a while' giving duration.
segments.s419-g28: برهنه is an adjective/adverb meaning 'naked' describing the subject's condition. سوی + place marks direction ('toward'); سیستانش is سیستان with the enclitic -ش marking possession (his Sīstān / his region) — here idiomatically 'toward Sīstān'. کشید is the past of کشیدن used intransitively in older poetic style meaning 'moved/drew (toward)'; the verb takes سوی + place to indicate direction.
segments.s419-g29: پذیرفت is the past tense 'accepted' (transitive). سامش is سام (the name Sam) with the enclitic -ش; here the suffix functions as the object pronoun ('him' — i.e. 'Sam accepted him') in the compact poetic word order (verb + subject+object‑suffix). ز introduces the reason 'because of' and begins the following causal list.
segments.s419-g30: This segment is a sequence of reasons introduced by the preposition ز 'from/because of'. نادانی 'ignorance' and دیو 'demon' name qualities; the element ی is a bound suffix forming an adjectival/relational form (here making 'دِیوی' = 'demonic' in the poem). غرچگی is another noun/quality (snarling or growling) completing the list. The coordinating و 'and' links the items.
segments.s419-g31: خجسته is an epithet 'blessed/auspicious' modifying the following nouns. بزرگان can mean 'great ones' or 'forebears' (here familiarly 'my elders/ancestors'); و links to شاهان 'kings'. من at the end is a possessive/expressive ('my') used poetically after the noun to address or invoke them ('my kings').
segments.s419-g32: نیای من = 'my ancestor(s)' (نیای is 'ancestor'); و joins نیکخواهان من 'my well‑wishers' (نیکخواهان = well‑wishers, benevolent ones). Again من is used poetically as a possessive after the nouns. The line is vocative/praise, addressing these groups.

sentences.s419-s1: Thus Esfandiyār spoke to Rostam: “O kind‑hearted, renowned chief!”
sentences.s419-s2: I have heard this from the wise — from the great men and the wakeful‑hearted mobeds (priests).
sentences.s419-s3: From them (I heard) that your ancestors, long‑departed, were proud, devout, and pure.
sentences.s419-s4: that no demon‑born of evil nature has a lineage in the world that surpasses (yours).
sentences.s419-s5: They kept many things hidden from Sam, and continually kept an uprising of the world.
sentences.s419-s6: His body was dark and ill‑haired and his face white; when Sam saw him, Sam's heart became hopeless.
sentences.s419-s7: He commanded that they carry (it) to the sea, so that perhaps the birds and the fish would thank it.
sentences.s419-s8: It came; the Simurgh spread its wing — in it he saw no rite nor glory.
sentences.s419-s9: He carried him to the place that was his bosom; from my hands the food for him was a satisfaction.
sentences.s419-s10: Although the Simorgh's lunch was small, Zal's body in its presence was despised.
sentences.s419-s11: He then threw him down before the bosom; at seeing him no one was glad.
sentences.s419-s12: He kept eating the carrion that had been thrown; his garment torn, his body naked, he was despised.
sentences.s419-s17: They lifted him up and gave him many things; that year also passed.
sentences.s419-s18: A certain cypress — its top unscarred — when it put forth a branch, Rostam came to it.
sentences.s419-s19: From his manliness and stature and his appearance, such a deed rose to the heavens.
sentences.s419-s20: Thus he obtained the kingship; he grew proud, and impiety seized him.
sentences.s419-s13: When the Simurgh cast its affection upon Zāl, the heavens turned thus for a while.
sentences.s419-s14: After that, having tasted carrion for a while, he went naked toward Sīstān.
sentences.s419-s15: Sam accepted him because of childlessness, ignorance, a demonic nature, and snarling.
sentences.s419-s16: Blessed be my great ones and my kings, my ancestors and my well‑wishers.
